quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Moment of inertia (discrete)"?

options.text: A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work

quiz_questions.explanation: "Moment of inertia (discrete)" is defined as: I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Moment of inertia (continuous)"?

options.text: I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object
An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Moment of inertia (continuous)" is defined as: I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Parallel axis theorem"?

options.text: I = I_cm + Md²; the moment of inertia about any axis equals I about the center of mass plus Md²
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
T² = (4π²/GM)r³; orbital period squared is proportional to orbital radius cubed
P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second

quiz_questions.explanation: "Parallel axis theorem" is defined as: I = I_cm + Md²; the moment of inertia about any axis equals I about the center of mass plus Md². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Common moments of inertia"?

options.text: An inclined road surface where the normal force has a radial component, providing centripetal force without relying solely on friction
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
Solid cylinder: ½MR², hoop: MR², solid sphere: ⅖MR², rod (center): 1/12 ML², rod (end): ⅓ML²

quiz_questions.explanation: "Common moments of inertia" is defined as: Solid cylinder: ½MR², hoop: MR², solid sphere: ⅖MR², rod (center): 1/12 ML², rod (end): ⅓ML². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rotational kinetic energy"?

options.text: Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves
KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rotational kinetic energy" is defined as: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis"?

options.text: Newton's Second Law for rotation
Moment of inertia (discrete)
Center of mass motion
Normal force on incline

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Moment of inertia (discrete)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "I = ∫r² dm; requires integration over the mass distribution of the object"?

options.text: Geosynchronous orbit
Moment of inertia (continuous)
Gravitational PE (universal)
Angular frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Moment of inertia (continuous)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "I = I_cm + Md²; the moment of inertia about any axis equals I about the center of mass plus Md²"?

options.text: Parallel axis theorem
Tangential velocity
Critical speed
Non-uniform circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Parallel axis theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Solid cylinder: ½MR², hoop: MR², solid sphere: ⅖MR², rod (center): 1/12 ML², rod (end): ⅓ML²"?

options.text: Common moments of inertia
Static equilibrium
Transverse vs. longitudinal
Linear momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Common moments of inertia".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation"?

options.text: Dot product
Free-body diagram
Rotational kinetic energy
Apparent weight

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rotational kinetic energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Rotational Inertia is accurate?

options.text: Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.
Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.
Rotational Inertia (moment of inertia, I) quantifies an object's resistance to angular acceleration, analogous to mass in linear motion.
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Inertia as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Rotational Inertia?

options.text: For discrete particles, I = Σm_i r_i², and for continuous bodies, I = ∫r² dm.
In AP Physics C, the calculus formulation F = dp/dt can be applied to systems with time-varying forces, including rocket propulsion where mass changes continuously.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Inertia as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Rotational Inertia?

options.text: The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Inertia as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Rotational Inertia?

options.text: This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
Standard formulas exist for common shapes (rod, disk, sphere, hoop) that appear frequently on the AP exam.
Energy oscillates between kinetic (½mv²) and potential (½kx²) with total energy E = ½kA² constant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Inertia as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Rotational Inertia?

options.text: The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed for maintaining contact) occurs when the normal force equals zero: mg = mv²/r, giving v_min = √(gr).
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Rotational Inertia (moment of inertia, I) quantifies an object's resistance to angular acceleration, analogous to mass in linear motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Rotational Inertia as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia?

options.text: Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)
Stellar evolution: collapsing gas clouds spin faster as they shrink (I decreases → ω increases), explaining the rapid rotation of neutron stars
Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides
Rocket equation derivation: integrating F = dp/dt for variable mass to find the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation Δv = v_e × ln(m₀/m_f)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Rotational Inertia?

options.text: Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Amusement park ride engineering ensuring loop-the-loop roller coasters maintain sufficient speed at the top for the normal force to remain positive
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Rotational Inertia applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Engine crankshaft design optimizing moment of inertia to smooth out power delivery from individual cylinder firings
Tire engineering optimizing rubber compounds to maximize μ_s for different road conditions (dry, wet, icy) for vehicle safety
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Rotational Inertia?

options.text: Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)
Earthquake-resistant building design using tuned mass dampers (large SHM systems) to counteract the building's oscillation and reduce structural damage
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Rotational Inertia in practice?

options.text: Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction
Baseball physics analyzing the impulse of a bat on a ball from high-speed camera force-time data to optimize hitting mechanics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Rotational Inertia.
